1.text: The United States Constitution outlines the powers of the federal government, including taxation, commerce regulation, naturalization, and currency management. All duties, imposts, and excises are uniform throughout the country. The government has the authority to borrow money, regulate commerce with foreign nations and among states, establish a uniform rule of naturalization, and fix standards for weights and measures. Additionally, the Constitution authorizes the establishment of post offices, promotion of science and arts through exclusive rights for authors and inventors, and the creation of tribunals inferior to the supreme court. The government is also responsible for punishing counterfeiting, defining and punishing piracy and felonies on the high seas, declaring war, and maintaining a navy. Furthermore, Congress has the power to raise and support armies, make rules for land and naval forces, and provide for organizing and arming the militia.

This document outlines provisions for the commencement of proceedings under the newly ratified Constitution, specifically regarding the appointment and voting process for electors, senators, and representatives. The passage establishes that after publication, electors should be appointed, and senators and representatives elected on a fixed date, with their votes to be transmitted certified. The terms of office for the President, Vice President, Senators, and Representatives are tied to the existing calendar, with their original end dates adjusted to January 3rd, and their successors' terms beginning immediately after. This provision also stipulates that Congress shall assemble at least once annually, commencing on January 3rd unless otherwise specified by law.

The United States Constitution establishes the legislative branch of government, vesting all powers in a Congress comprising a Senate and House of Representatives. The House of Representatives is composed of members elected every second year by state electors, who must meet specific qualifications, including age and residency requirements. Members must be at least 25 years old, have been U.S. citizens for seven years, and reside in the state they represent. The number of representatives allocated to each state will be determined by its population, with a minimum of one representative per state. The actual census will be conducted every ten years, starting within three years after the first Congress meeting, and will determine the allocation of representatives based on the total number of free persons, including those bound to service for a term of years, while excluding Indians not taxed and three-fifths of all other persons.

The Constitution of the United States is a foundational document adopted by the People of the United States on September 17, 1787, and ratified on June 21, 1788. The Preamble outlines the purpose of the Constitution: to form a more perfect Union, establish Justice, insure domestic Tranquility, provide for the common defence, promote the general Welfare, and secure the Blessings of Liberty to the citizens and their Posterity. Article I of the Constitution establishes the legislative branch of the federal government, comprising Congress, which is divided into two houses: the House of Representatives and the Senate. The Constitution also outlines the principles of federalism, dividing power between the national government and the states. The document's authors, including James Madison, George Washington, and Benjamin Franklin, drew inspiration from various philosophical and historical sources, including the English Bill of Rights and the Magna Carta. The Constitution has undergone 27 amendments since its ratification, with the most recent being the 27th Amendment in 1992.

The United States Constitution outlines the framework for federal and state governments, with Articles III-IV detailing the judicial branch's role in interpreting laws and resolving disputes. Article III establishes the Supreme Court as the highest court in the land, with jurisdiction over cases involving federal laws and treaties, as well as disputes between states (Article III, Section 2). The Judiciary Act of 1789 established the number of circuit judges at six, with each circuit covering a specific geographic area (Article III, Section 1). In Article IV, the Constitution outlines the process for admitting new states to the Union and establishes the principle of federalism, dividing power between the national government and individual states. The article also requires states to respect the laws and citizens of other states, with provisions governing extradition, citizenship, and the protection of rights (Article IV, Sections 2-5). Notably, Article III and IV differ in their focus on judicial versus executive powers, reflecting the Constitution's system of checks and balances.

The House of Representatives chooses its Speaker and other officers, exercising sole power of impeachment. In contrast, the Senate is composed of two senators from each state, chosen by their respective legislatures for six-year terms. Senators are divided into three classes, with one-third up for election every second year, except in cases of resignation or vacancy during a state's legislative recess, when the executive may make temporary appointments until the next legislature meeting. The Senate and Representatives receive compensation for their services, ascertained by law and paid from the US Treasury. They enjoy certain privileges, including immunity from arrest during sessions and protection against questioning for speeches or debates in either house. Additionally, no senator or representative can be appointed to a civil office under the US authority created or emoluments increased during their term, nor can individuals holding such offices serve in either house while in office.

The document outlines the initial allocation of representatives in the United States Congress, with each state assigned a specific number of seats based on its population. The House of Representatives is responsible for choosing their own members, while vacancies are filled through executive authority issuing writs of election. This provision applies to both civil and criminal cases, with judgments limited to removal from office and disqualification from future public service, but not affecting liability for prosecution or punishment.

The Constitution also grants Congress exclusive jurisdiction over a ten-mile square district that becomes the seat of government by cession or acceptance, as well as authority over places purchased by state legislatures for military purposes. Additionally, the document includes the presidential oath of office, which requires the President to faithfully execute their duties and preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution of the United States.

The passages collectively describe the conditions under which a proposed constitutional amendment shall be operative. Specifically, they outline the requirement for ratification by three-fourths of the states within a seven-year period following submission to the states by Congress. The exact wording and formatting of the sections differ, but their content is identical. Amendment XXI is mentioned in both instances, with its passage date noted as February 20, 1933, and ratification date as December 5, 1933. Notably, one section (SECTION 6) includes Amendment XXI's details twice, while the other (SECTION 3) provides a similar but distinct description of the amendment's operational conditions.

The amendments to the United States Constitution, proposed and ratified between 1791 and 1865, comprise a cluster of provisions aimed at expanding individual rights and clarifying federal authority. The Bill of Rights (Amendments I-X), ratified in 1791, guarantees freedoms such as freedom of religion and speech, while Amendment IX ensures that unenumerated rights are retained by the people. Amendments XI-XXVII address various aspects of federal jurisdiction, including the judicial power of the United States (XI) and the electoral process for President and Vice-President (XII). The 11th Amendment restricts federal court jurisdiction over suits against states by citizens of other states or foreign nationals, while the 12th Amendment modifies Article II, Section 1 to ensure separate elections for President and Vice-President. Amendments XIII-XXVII further refine federal authority, including the abolition of slavery (XIII) and clarifying the relationship between states and the federal government.

The United States Constitution establishes the Senate as the upper house of the bicameral legislature, with specific eligibility requirements for senators. To be eligible, a person must have attained the age of 30 years, been a citizen of the United States for at least nine years, and reside in the state they represent when elected. The Vice President serves as President of the Senate but has no voting power unless tied. The Senate chooses its other officers, including a President pro tempore, to preside in the absence of the Vice President or when they assume the office of President. The Senate holds sole power to try impeachments, with the Chief Justice presiding over trials involving the President and requiring a two-thirds majority for conviction. Convictions result in removal from office and disqualification from future offices, but do not affect liability for other crimes. The Constitution also outlines the executive branch, vesting the president's power during a four-year term, which is elected concurrently with the Vice President.

The Senate and House of Representatives are composed of two Senators from each State, chosen by the Legislature for six years, with one vote per Senator. The Congress assembles at least once a year, typically on the first Monday in December, unless otherwise specified by law. Each state prescribes its own regulations for holding elections for Senators and Representatives, which can be altered or made by Congress except for the places of choosing Senators. The Congress is divided into three classes with staggered terms: one-third expire every two years, one-third every four years, and one-third every six years.

Both Houses are responsible for their own elections, returns, and qualifications, with a majority required to do business. A smaller number can adjourn from day to day and compel absent members' attendance under specified penalties. Each House determines its rules of procedure, punishes disorderly behavior, and expels members with two-thirds concurrence. Both Houses keep a journal of their proceedings, publishing them except for parts requiring secrecy, and record the yeas and nays on questions upon request from one-fifth of present members. Neither House can adjourn without the other's consent for more than three days or to another location.

The United States Constitution outlines the process for electing the President and Vice President. Each state appoints a number of electors equal to its total number of Senators and Representatives, with no senator or representative holding an office under the US being eligible as an elector. Electors meet in their respective states, vote by ballot for two candidates, and submit a list of votes certified and signed. The President of the Senate opens the certificates, and the votes are counted in the presence of the Senate and House of Representatives. If one candidate has a majority of votes, they become President; if not, the House of Representatives chooses between the top five candidates on the list by state vote. A quorum requires two-thirds of states represented, with a majority necessary for a choice. The person with the most votes becomes Vice President, unless there is a tie, in which case the Senate chooses from the tied candidates.

2.text: The United States Constitution outlines the powers and framework of the federal government, including taxation, commerce regulation, naturalization, currency management, and the establishment of post offices, tribunals, and a navy. Adopted on September 17, 1787, and ratified on June 21, 1788, the document authorizes Congress to borrow money, regulate commerce with foreign nations and among states, establish uniform rules for naturalization, weights, and measures, and punish counterfeiting and piracy. The Constitution also establishes the legislative branch, comprising Congress, which is divided into two houses: the House of Representatives and the Senate. Article III details the judicial branch's role in interpreting laws and resolving disputes, establishing the Supreme Court as the highest court in the land. The document has undergone 27 amendments since its ratification, with notable changes including the addition of the 27th Amendment in 1992. Notably, Articles I, III, and IV differ in their focus on legislative, judicial, and executive powers, respectively, reflecting the Constitution's system of checks and balances.

The document outlines provisions for the commencement of proceedings under the newly ratified Constitution, specifically regarding the appointment and voting process for electors, senators, and representatives. The terms of office for the President, Vice President, Senators, and Representatives are tied to the existing calendar, with their original end dates adjusted to January 3rd, and their successors' terms beginning immediately after. This provision also stipulates that Congress shall assemble at least once annually, commencing on January 3rd unless otherwise specified by law.

The Constitution establishes a bicameral legislature comprising a Senate and House of Representatives, vesting all powers in Congress. The House of Representatives is composed of members elected every second year by state electors, with specific qualifications including age, residency requirements, and citizenship status. Senators are chosen by their respective legislatures for six-year terms, with two senators from each state.

The document also establishes the Senate as the upper house, with eligibility requirements including age 30, U.S. citizenship for nine years, and residency in the state represented. The Vice President serves as President of the Senate but has no voting power unless tied. The Senate holds sole power to try impeachments, with the Chief Justice presiding over trials involving the President.

The Constitution also outlines the executive branch, vesting the president's power during a four-year term elected concurrently with the Vice President. Members of Congress receive compensation for their services, ascertained by law and paid from the US Treasury, while enjoying certain privileges including immunity from arrest and protection against questioning for speeches or debate
